patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old female warehouse shift supervisor. You speak vaguely and try to keep answers short; you’re a bit evasive, especially about anything that could end up “on record.” Start by saying: “My feet keep turning in and I keep tripping—it's getting worse.” Volunteer only: frequent tripping (uneven ground/stairs), ankles feel weak/roll easily, you can’t lift your toes well so your feet “slap,” shoes wear out on the outside edge, and your arches have gotten very high over time. patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old warehouse picker. You sound vague and a bit evasive, focused on getting the nosebleed to stop fast so you can get back to work. Start by volunteering: the nosebleed has been on-and-off for hours and restarts, you’re getting big bruises from tiny bumps or no clear injury, your gums bleed when brushing, and you’ve been more tired lately.

If asked directly, admit: very heavy week-long periods with clots/soaking, cuts ooze a long time, you bled a lot after dental work, some bruises become painful lumps, headaches/paleness around heavy bleeding.

Withhold unless specifically probed: long history since childhood, avoiding sports, pink urine after bad nosebleed days, joint swelling after minor bumps, and family “bleeding issues.”

Resist bringing up: bleeding after sex, rectal bleeding after constipation, and thigh/upper arm bruises (fear judgment). Offer misconceptions: “dry air/allergies,” “maybe iron/red meat,” “birth control clotting,” “just gum disease.” If met with empathy, soften and share more, but ask about cost, time off, and whether this means lifelong infusions.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using both terms: explicitly states 'nephritis' and clarifies it means 'kidney inflammation'.

Provides a safety-net statement describing red flags that warrant urgent/emergency evaluation (e.g., reduced urine output, severe swelling, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe hypertension, confusion, rapidly worsening symptoms).
Before any prescribing, performs and/or documents an allergy check and a contraindication check (critical failures if omitted).
Performs and/or documents a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical if omitted).
Explains treatment plan in plain language and explicitly instructs the patient to avoid NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen/naproxen) due to kidney risk.
Avoids recommending nephrotoxic medications and avoids giving NSAIDs for pain/fever; suggests safer alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen within safe dosing) when needed.
Acknowledges uncertainty of nephritis subtype and outlines need for further evaluation (e.g., urine studies, renal function monitoring, serologies, imaging) and nephrology input.
Assesses and communicates pregnancy status/teratogenic risk considerations when prescribing potentially harmful medications (if patient is of childbearing potential).
Uses shared decision-making and confirms patient understanding of diagnosis, plan, and follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose nephritis, initiate kidney-safe initial management with required safety checks, and urgently refer to nephrology.
must_collect: recent infection (sore throat/skin infection) or systemic symptoms (fever, rash, joint pain)
medication exposures (especially NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs) and OTC/herbals
contraindications to proposed therapy (e.g., active infection, GI bleed history for steroids, uncontrolled diabetes/HTN considerations)
steps: Identify presentation consistent with nephritis and communicate that it is kidney inflammation.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; perform drug_interaction_check when feasible.
Start kidney-protective management and explicitly counsel to avoid NSAIDs; select medications compatible with kidney disease and patient context.
Arrange urgent nephrology referral for definitive workup/possible biopsy and specialist-directed treatment.
Provide return precautions and near-term follow-up/monitoring plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Supportive-only initial treatment while deferring immunosuppression to nephrology, still with NSAID avoidance and safety checks if any medication is prescribed.
steps: Record nephritis diagnosis with explanation of kidney inflammation.
If prescribing any medication (e.g., antihypertensive, diuretic, acid suppression for steroid prophylaxis), complete allergy and contraindication checks first and consider interaction check.
Counsel avoid NSAIDs and other nephrotoxins; provide hydration/low-salt guidance as appropriate.
Urgently refer to nephrology for etiology evaluation and definitive therapy.
Safety-net and follow-up plan.

acceptable_variants.description: If features suggest drug-induced interstitial nephritis, prioritize stopping the offending agent and refer urgently, with cautious use of steroids only after contraindication screening.
steps: Record nephritis diagnosis and discuss possible medication-related kidney inflammation.
Review recent medication exposures and advise stopping suspected offending drug(s) when safe to do so.
Before any steroid prescription, complete allergy and contraindication checks (and interaction check if feasible).
Counsel avoid NSAIDs; provide symptomatic alternatives.
Urgently refer to nephrology for confirmation and management; safety-net and monitoring.
